skill.name: datadata-api
skill.description: 通过 CLI 查询 Datadata — 运行临时 SQL、检查数据源元数据、列表和描述表、下载 NDJSON/CSV 格式结果。当用户提到 Datadata、想查询数据、探索数据源或表 schema、获取查询结果时使用此 skill。触发：Datadata、数据探索、SQL查询、表检查、执行结果。
skill.summary: 通过 CLI 查询 Datadata — 运行临时 SQL、检查数据源元数据、列表和描述表、下载 NDJSON/CSV 格式结果。当用户提到 Datadata、想查询数据、探索数据源或表 schema...
skill.author: datadata-team

security_audit.summary: This skill is a legitimate API client for the Datadata platform. All 815 static findings are false positives resulting from documentation code examples in markdown files (backtick shell commands), standard API client patterns (HTTP requests, credential storage), and heuristic entropy detection on legitimate content. The Python CLI script is well-structured, uses stdlib only, stores credentials with proper filesystem permissions (0700/0600), and connects only to the known datadata.com domain. No evidence of malicious intent, obfuscation, or data exfiltration was found.

medium_findings.title: External command execution in documentation examples
medium_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE: The static analyzer detected backtick patterns in reference markdown files and SKILL.md as Ruby/shell execution. These are shell command examples (curl, CLI invocations) shown in code blocks for documentation purposes. They are not executable code within the skill itself. The actual CLI execution happens through the agent running python3 scripts/datadata_query.py as a subprocess, which is the expected operation of this skill.

medium_findings.title: Ruby shell backtick patterns in agent YAML
medium_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE: The openai.yaml agent definition includes backtick-delimited code examples demonstrating CLI usage patterns. These are instructional examples for the AI agent, not executable Ruby/shell code. The skill is designed to run datadata_query.py as a Python subprocess, which is its legitimate function.

low_findings.title: API credential access in legitimate API client
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE: The script reads DATADATA_API_KEY from environment variables and a local config file (~/.config/datadata/config.json). This is expected behavior for a legitimate API client tool. Credentials are stored with secure filesystem permissions (0600). The access pattern is not malicious - it is the standard way CLI tools handle authentication.

low_findings.title: Network HTTP libraries in API client
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE: The script imports urllib.request and makes HTTP requests to api.datadata.com endpoints. This is expected behavior for an API client tool. The script connects only to a single known domain (datadata.com) for legitimate data query operations, with no evidence of data exfiltration or connections to unknown endpoints.

low_findings.title: Weak cryptographic algorithm detection on datetime import
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE: The static analyzer flagged import datetime as a weak cryptographic algorithm. The datetime module is used for timestamp calculations (API key expiry checks, device flow state expiration). No actual cryptographic operations are performed. This is a standard library import with no security implications.

low_findings.title: Hidden file access for credential storage
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE: The script stores API keys in ~/.config/datadata/config.json with proper Unix permissions (0700 for directory, 0600 for file). This is the standard and recommended practice for CLI tools to persist credentials. The access pattern is not suspicious or malicious.

low_findings.title: System reconnaissance detection on whoami API call
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE: The script includes a whoami command that calls a Datadata API endpoint (/api/v1/api-keys/whoami) to verify API key validity and inspect permissions. This is not Unix system reconnaissance. The static analyzer confused the API endpoint name with the Unix whoami command.

dangerous_patterns.title: High entropy heuristic (false positive)
dangerous_patterns.description: FALSE POSITIVE: The entropy heuristic flagged high file entropy in query-guide.md (6.55 bits) and SKILL.md (6.42 bits). These are legitimate markdown documentation files containing code examples with UUIDs, identifiers, and structured data. The entropy values are consistent with text files containing mixed natural language and code, not encrypted or obfuscated content.

dangerous_patterns.title: Combined risk factor heuristic (false positive)
dangerous_patterns.description: FALSE POSITIVE: The static analyzer flagged a dangerous combination of code execution + network + credential access. However, this combination is expected for a legitimate API client tool: it runs as a CLI subprocess, makes HTTP requests to a single known API domain, and reads API credentials securely. No evidence of malicious exfiltration or unauthorized access patterns was found.

dangerous_patterns.title: Path traversal in documentation examples
dangerous_patterns.description: FALSE POSITIVE: Path traversal sequences were detected in reference/api.md. These are example file paths shown in API documentation code examples, not actual filesystem operations. The actual Python script uses tempfile.gettempdir() for output file paths, preventing traversal attacks.

content.user_title: Query and explore data with Datadata SQL API
content.value_statement: Data analysts and engineers waste time switching between database consoles and writing boilerplate API clients. This skill lets you query any Datadata datasource, explore table schemas, and download results using natural language commands via a CLI tool.
actual_capabilities: Search public and private datasources on Datadata using keyword or owner/name queries
List tables and describe column schemas for any datasource
Execute ad-hoc SELECT queries with DuckDB or ClickHouse engine support
Download query results in NDJSON or CSV format to local files
Create tables, insert batch data, and drop tables in ducklake data spaces
Authenticate via device authorization flow or API key with permission verification
limitations: Supports only SELECT queries - INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE and DDL statements are not allowed
Query results can be very large; full dataset is never loaded into context automatically
Cross-datasource joins are only available with the DuckDB query engine
Datasource search results require manual user confirmation before use

use_cases.title: Quick data exploration for analysts
use_cases.description: A data analyst needs to explore a new datasource. They ask to see available tables, inspect column types, and run sample queries without opening a separate SQL client.
use_cases.target_user: Data analysts and business intelligence professionals

use_cases.title: Ad-hoc SQL queries for engineers
use_cases.description: A software engineer needs to run a one-time SQL query across multiple datasources for debugging or validation. They describe the query in natural language and get results in their preferred format.

use_cases.title: Data ingestion and management
use_cases.description: A data engineer needs to create a staging table, ingest CSV data, and verify the results before downstream processing. The skill handles table creation, data insertion, and schema verification.
use_cases.target_user: Data engineers and data pipeline operators

prompt_templates.title: Search and explore a datasource
prompt_templates.prompt: Search for datasources named 'sales' on Datadata, list their tables, and describe the columns of the most relevant table.
prompt_templates.scenario: A user wants to find data and understand its structure before running queries.

prompt_templates.title: Run a SQL query
prompt_templates.prompt: Query the 'orders' table in the 'analytics' datasource to find the top 10 customers by total purchase amount in the last 30 days. Use DuckDB engine and return results as CSV.
prompt_templates.scenario: A user needs specific data from a known datasource with filtering and aggregation.

prompt_templates.title: Cross-datasource analysis
prompt_templates.prompt: Join the 'customers' table from datasource A with the 'orders' table from datasource B using DuckDB engine. Find customers who have not placed an order in 90 days.
prompt_templates.scenario: A user needs to correlate data across multiple datasources for advanced analysis.

prompt_templates.title: Set up a data space table
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a table called 'daily_metrics' in my ducklake data space with columns for date, metric_name, and metric_value. Then insert the following rows: [list of rows]. Finally describe the table to confirm it was created correctly.
prompt_templates.scenario: A user wants to persist processed data for later querying in a data space.

output_examples.input: Show me the tables in the 'analytics' datasource and describe their columns.
output: Found datasource 'analytics' with 3 tables: daily_revenue, user_sessions, and product_catalog.
Table 'daily_revenue' has columns: date (DATE), revenue (DECIMAL), transactions (INTEGER), and avg_order_value (DECIMAL).
Table 'user_sessions' has columns: session_id (STRING), user_id (STRING), started_at (TIMESTAMP), duration_seconds (INTEGER).
Table 'product_catalog' has columns: product_id (STRING), name (STRING), category (STRING), price (DECIMAL), stock (INTEGER).

output_examples.input: Run a query to find the top 5 products by revenue this month.
output: Query executed successfully. Execution ID: exec_Kd8sH3jfLm2p.
Preview: Product A ($12,450), Product B ($9,230), Product C ($7,810), Product D ($5,430), Product E ($3,210).
Use get-execution-result to download the full file or run further analysis.

best_practices: Always inspect table schemas with list-tables and describe-table before writing SQL queries to ensure column names and types are correct
Use the DuckDB query engine for cross-datasource joins and reserve ClickHouse engine only for ClickHouse-native datasources
Query result files should be examined with head, tail, or grep commands instead of loading the entire file into conversation context
anti_patterns: Do not automatically select a datasource from search results without user confirmation - this can lead to querying or writing to the wrong target
Do not chain multiple operations without user instruction - complete one step and wait for the next command to avoid acting on unverified assumptions
Do not read entire result files into context - files can be hundreds of megabytes and will exhaust available context window

faq.question: How do I get an API key for Datadata?
faq.answer: Use the device authorization flow: run device-flow-start, open the provided URL in a browser to authorize, then run device-flow-complete. The key is saved automatically. Alternatively, create one manually in Datadata Settings under API Keys.

faq.question: What SQL engines are supported?
faq.answer: DuckDB is the default engine and supports cross-datasource joins. ClickHouse engine is available for ClickHouse-native datasources. Always use DuckDB SQL syntax unless the datasource requires ClickHouse.

faq.question: Can I modify data with this tool?
faq.answer: The execute-adhoc command only supports SELECT queries. For data modification, use the create-table, insert-rows, and drop-table commands which work with ducklake data spaces.

faq.question: How do I handle large query results?
faq.answer: Execution results are downloaded to local files. Use command-line tools like head, tail, wc -l, grep, or awk to inspect and process large files instead of loading them into the conversation.

faq.question: What permissions does my API key need?
faq.answer: Minimum permissions are queries:execute-adhoc, executions:get, datasources:read, and datasources:scan. For data space operations, also add data-spaces:write.

faq.question: How long is my API key valid?
faq.answer: Device-authorized keys expire after 90 days. Expired keys are automatically detected and cleared from the config file. You will be prompted to re-authorize when needed.
